original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what issue was resolved in DHCP Snooping Bug 325316?
original_answer: DHCP Snooping (Bug ID 325316): Symptom: A critical log message is generated for a ipsavd process daemon restart with signal 11. Scenario: This daemon restart will be observed when a DHCP client sends a DHCP renew packet without the end option when dhcpv4-snooping option 82 is disabled. Workaround: Enable dhcpv4-snooping option 82, or configure a maximum lease-time for the client (365 days) to avoid this issue.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what issue was resolved in Spanning Tree Bug 329044?
original_answer: Spanning Tree (Bug ID 329044): Symptom: An RPVST daemon daemon restart occurred. Scenario: This issue can occur when connecting an STP-aware switch to a port where spanning-tree guard is configured. Ensure that the peer switch has an RPVST VLAN that is not configured on the switch. Workaround: Refrain from connecting STP-aware switches to these ports. Alternatively, temporarily remove the BPDU-guard configuration to prevent further daemon restarts.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what known issue is documented for RADIUS Port-Access Bug 320342?
original_answer: RADIUS Port-Access (Bug ID 320342): Symptom: The RADIUS server hostname will get updated to the renewed IP addresses found in DNS server. Scenario: The problem is seen when the host addresses in DNS servers get updated against the hostnames, but the Switch fails to refresh its cache with the renewed address. Workaround: The default refresh timer configured in the switch is 300 seconds. A network administrator can configure a custom value according to their needs after which the switch would automatically update the host addresses of the RADIUS server in its cache.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what known issue is documented for NTP Bug 322650?
original_answer: NTP (Bug ID 322650): Symptom: NTP entries are not updating correctly with dhcpv4 and v6 client enable/disable Scenario 1: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 enabled and DHCPv6 disabled: Disabling DHCPv4 and enabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected When both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 disabled and DHCPv6 enabled: Enabling DHCPv4 and disabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Scenario 2: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the different VLANs: Re-enabling DHCP mode on lower interface VLAN does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, BL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what issue was resolved in DHCP Snooping Bug 325316?
original_answer: DHCP Snooping (Bug ID 325316): Symptom: A critical log message is generated for a ipsavd process daemon restart with signal 11. Scenario: This daemon restart will be observed when a DHCP client sends a DHCP renew packet without the end option when dhcpv4-snooping option 82 is disabled. Workaround: Enable dhcpv4-snooping option 82, or configure a maximum lease-time for the client (365 days) to avoid this issue.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what issue was resolved in Spanning Tree Bug 329044?
original_answer: Spanning Tree (Bug ID 329044): Symptom: An RPVST daemon daemon restart occurred. Scenario: This issue can occur when connecting an STP-aware switch to a port where spanning-tree guard is configured. Ensure that the peer switch has an RPVST VLAN that is not configured on the switch. Workaround: Refrain from connecting STP-aware switches to these ports. Alternatively, temporarily remove the BPDU-guard configuration to prevent further daemon restarts.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what known issue is documented for RADIUS Port-Access Bug 320342?
original_answer: RADIUS Port-Access (Bug ID 320342): Symptom: The RADIUS server hostname will get updated to the renewed IP addresses found in DNS server. Scenario: The problem is seen when the host addresses in DNS servers get updated against the hostnames, but the Switch fails to refresh its cache with the renewed address. Workaround: The default refresh timer configured in the switch is 300 seconds. A network administrator can configure a custom value according to their needs after which the switch would automatically update the host addresses of the RADIUS server in its cache.

original_answer: NTP (Bug ID 322650): Symptom: NTP entries are not updating correctly with dhcpv4 and v6 client enable/disable Scenario 1: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 enabled and DHCPv6 disabled: Disabling DHCPv4 and enabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected When both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 disabled and DHCPv6 enabled: Enabling DHCPv4 and disabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Scenario 2: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the different VLANs: Re-enabling DHCP mode on lower interface VLAN does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, BL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1005, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1005, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1005, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1005, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1005, what known issue is documented for NTP Bug 322650?
original_answer: NTP (Bug ID 322650): Symptom: NTP entries are not updating correctly with dhcpv4 and v6 client enable/disable Scenario 1: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 enabled and DHCPv6 disabled: Disabling DHCPv4 and enabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected When both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 disabled and DHCPv6 enabled: Enabling DHCPv4 and disabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Scenario 2: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the different VLANs: Re-enabling DHCP mode on lower interface VLAN does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1005, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, BL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1005, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1005, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_answer: Feature Caveat: Client IP Tracker - The Client IP tracker tracks IP addresses until IP source lockdown is applied. After lockdown, only valid binding table entries are tracked. Already tracked IPs remain active through ARP probes, which use Layer 2 packets.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what issue was resolved in Mirroring Bug 342437?
original_answer: Mirroring (Bug ID 342437): Symptom: Packets near MTU limits in size may not be present in a port or tunnel/remote based mirror. Scenario: When a client configures a mirror where the destination interface has the same MTU size, mirrored packets that are close to max MTU are incorrectly dropped and not mirrored. This applies to remote/tunnel mirrors as well. Workaround: Configuring mirror destination ports as routing port and set the IP MTU size. For a VLAN interface, set the IP MTU on the VLAN SVI. IP MTU must be set to accommodate the ethernet overhead +tag. For the default MTU, 1500, the IP MTU would be 1518. For a tunneled scenario, the MTU must also account for the tunnel header.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what issue was resolved in MAC Table Bug 340999?
original_answer: MAC Table (Bug ID 340999): Symptom: The switch doesnt learn the MAC of a client that is denied on a secure port even when its moved to a trusted/non-secure port. Scenario: Configure port-access on a port (MAC_Auth/Dot1x/port-security). Setup the client MAC that is trying to onboard such that the MAC is denied. For example: Either by failing authentication or due to max client limit. Move such a client to a non-secure port. Observe the MAC table that the MAC is not moved to the non-secure port.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what issue was resolved in VXLAN Bug 339717?
original_answer: VXLAN (Bug ID 339717): Symptom: ops-switchd crash while collecting VXLAN diagdump or collecting support files all. Scenario: In some VXLAN scenarios with multiple LAGs and multiple overlay VLAN on those lags. After device reboot, the LAG interface could show incorrect VXLAN Programming. If the user attempts to remove VLANs, VNI-to-VXLAN configurations that belong to the affected LAGS, a ops-switchd crash might be observed. Workaround: Ensure LAG interfaces are up before configuring the VLANs for the lag.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what issue was resolved in VXLAN Bug 339717?

If the user attempts to remove VLANs, VNI-to-VXLAN configurations that belong to the affected LAGs might cause a ops-switchd crash. Workaround: Ensure LAG interfaces are up before configuring the VLANs for the lag.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what issue was resolved in SNMP Bug 335246?
original_answer: SNMP (Bug ID 335246): Symptom: Delay of ~30s observed when updating VLAN access config via snmpset and verifying via snmpget immediately after. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what issue was resolved in ACL Bug 234200?
original_answer: ACL (Bug ID 234200): Symptom: ACL fails to apply only the VSX secondary switch. ACL warning logs display the ! access-list ip <name> user configuration does not match active configuration. ! error. Scenario: In some scenarios when a config checkpoint restore or classifier list-update is performed in a VSX member, the ACL config will not be applied to the VSX secondary if the ACL config has vsx-sync is enabled. Workaround: Remove the ACL configuration and then re-apply the ACL.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_answer: NTP (Bug ID 322650): Symptom: NTP entries are not updating correctly with dhcpv4 and v6 client enable/disable Scenario 1: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 enabled and DHCPv6 disabled: Disabling DHCPv4 and enabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected When both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 disabled and DHCPv6 enabled: Enabling DHCPv4 and disabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Scenario 2: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the different VLANs: Re-enabling DHCP mode on lower interface VLAN does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, BL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what known issue is documented for NTP Bug 322650?
original_answer: NTP (Bug ID 322650): Symptom: NTP entries are not updating correctly with dhcpv4 and v6 client enable/disable Scenario 1: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 enabled and DHCPv6 disabled: Disabling DHCPv4 and enabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected When both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 disabled and DHCPv6 enabled: Enabling DHCPv4 and disabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Scenario 2: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the different VLANs: Re-enabling DHCP mode on lower interface VLAN does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, BL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?

Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what known issue is documented for NTP Bug 322650?
original_answer: NTP (Bug ID 322650): Symptom: NTP entries are not updating correctly with dhcpv4 and v6 client enable/disable Scenario 1: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 enabled and DHCPv6 disabled: Disabling DHCPv4 and enabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected When both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 disabled and DHCPv6 enabled: Enabling DHCPv4 and disabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Scenario 2: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the different VLANs: Re-enabling DHCP mode on lower interface VLAN does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, BL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, BL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, what caveat is documented for Activate?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Activate - If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

reason: The original question and answer are clear but could be slightly refined for better readability.
original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, what known issue is documented for NTP Bug 322650?
original_answer: NTP (Bug ID 322650): Symptom: NTP entries are not updating correctly with dhcpv4 and v6 client enable/disable Scenario 1: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 enabled and DHCPv6 disabled: Disabling DHCPv4 and enabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected When both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the same VLAN with DHCPv4 disabled and DHCPv6 enabled: Enabling DHCPv4 and disabling DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected Scenario 2: When DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 clients are on the different VLANs: Re-enabling DHCP mode on lower interface VLAN does not update the NTP entries as expected Disabling both DHCPv4 and DHCPv6 does not update the NTP entries as expected.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, BL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, what are the steps to perform the software upgrade?

Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1060, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1060, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1060, what issue was resolved in Slot Management Bug 384894?
original_answer: Slot Management (Bug ID 384894): Symptom: Two network switches each contain a port with identical MAC addresses. Scenario: Two switches in the same network have adjacent base MAC addresses. Because of a flaw in the way that port MAC addresses are assigned in the system, Switch #1's port 1/1/2 is assigned an identical MAC address as Switch #2's base MAC address. This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.

This MAC address overlap within a network can cause automation processes to incorrectly identify both devices as the same entity, leading to operational issues. Workaround: Ensure that no two switches in a network have consecutive base MAC addresses, or do not use both of the ports with the overlap.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.15.1060, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.0002, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.0002, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.0002, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example,.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.0002, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.0002, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_answer: Feature Caveat: IP Source Lockdown - The IP source-lockdown feature allows client traffic through the switch only if their MAC and IP addresses are in the binding database. VLAN-level configuration overrides port-level configuration only when the port belongs to a single VLAN; otherwise, VLAN-level configuration is not applied.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what caveat is documented for MACsec?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - Ensure the cipher suite GCM-AES-128 is configured when AOS-CX is acting as a key server. By default, AOS-CX uses the more secure cipher suite GCM-AES-XPN-256 for establishing MACsec secure links. However, Comware/PVOS does not support the XPN cipher suite.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what caveat is documented for SNMP?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. For the primary controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the primary cluster. For the backup controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the backup cluster. Do not configure both primary and backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in Logging Bug 358847?
original_answer: Logging (Bug ID 358847): Symptom: A VSF stack member switch unexpectedly reboots due to the ops-switchd process crashing with a signal: 6 error. Scenario: In some scenarios with excessive ACL event logging, a crash may occur due to a failure in the system when processing a high volume of ACL log entries. Workaround: Minimize the use of the log keyword in ACL entries, especially on high-traffic rules. Consider using ACL counters instead of logging for monitoring traffic patterns.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in EVPN Bug 364576?
original_answer: EVPN (Bug ID 364576): Symptom: Missing ARP entries showing as "incomplete" in the ARP table, causing connectivity issues in remote VTEP. Scenario: This issue may occur in an EVPN VXLAN environment with VSX pairs when a VIP (Virtual IP) moves from one node to another node, MAC address aging occurs or after executing "clear mac-address" on the switch. Workaround: A temporary workaround is to add a static ARP entry for the affected IP addresses.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in L3 routes Bug 365274?
original_answer: L3 routes (Bug ID 365274): Symptom: Network Management Systems does not display IPv4 addresses for Aruba CX switches running AOS-CX version 10.13. Scenario: This issue occurs when the NMS queries the switch using SNMP and relies on either of the following OIDs to retrieve IPv4 address information: 1.3.6.1.2.1.4.20.1.2(deprecated) or1.3.6.1.2.1.4.34.1.5(current but not accessible in 10.13). original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example,.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what issue was resolved in SNMP Bug 400261?
original_answer: SNMP (Bug ID 400261): Symptom: SNMP OID sysUpTimeInstance (1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0) may return an incorrect value when polled, leading to inaccurate uptime reporting in monitoring systems. Scenario: This issue occurs on switches running AOS-CX 10.16 when using SNMP to query sysUpTimeInstance. The reported value does not match the actual system uptime, causing discrepancies in monitoring tools. Workaround: Use the OID hrSystemUptime (1.3.6.1.2.1.25.1.1.0) instead of sysUpTimeInstance for accurate system uptime reporting.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what issue was resolved in ARP Bug 404262?
original_answer: ARP (Bug ID 404262): Symptom: After switch reboot, keep alive neighbor may flap continuously and subsequent ARP resolution to random neighbors may fail. Scenario: When switch is configured with multiple VRF's and during reboot, internal sockets may get created before VRF namespace is ready. When this happens, there will be intermittent connectivity loss. Workaround: Remove and reconfigure the affected VRFs After a reboot or upgrade, port-security sticky clients may end up in a MAC denied state.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example,.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what caveat is documented for IP Source Lockdown?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: IP Source Lockdown - The IP source-lockdown feature allows client traffic through the switch only if their MAC and IP addresses are in the binding database. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example,.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, how do you perform the software upgrade?

1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.

3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for Routing?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Routing - Use a varied IPv6 address pattern across different hex segments to optimize the device’s ability to manage and store routes. The switch may fail to add routes even if the device route scale is 50% lower than the supported route scale if the IPv6 routes are contiguous, and only the fourth hexadecimal segment (the fourth “hex”) in the address is incremented. This addressing pattern (for example, 2000:1:1:1::/64, 2000:1:1:2::/64, etc.) may lead to inefficient memory or hardware resource utilization. To optimize hardware usage and avoid route failures, increment the third hexadecimal segment instead of the fourth. This adjustment supports route optimization by distributing routes more efficiently in hardware memory.

original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for SNMP?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. original_question: For 5420 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.
